title: When the Strategy Fails
subtitle: Pivots, escalation & course correction

hook.metaphor: Every sailor eventually finds themselves on the wrong heading. The good ones notice early and turn; the doomed ones, certain they're right, sail harder in the wrong direction. The hardest part of strategy isn't choosing the course — it's admitting when it's wrong and changing it, against every instinct telling you to push on.
hook.scenario: A company's flagship strategy clearly isn't working — losses mount, the market keeps shrinking. But leadership has staked its reputation on it, so it doubles down again and again, pouring in more money to prove it right. Each step deeper makes turning back harder. What trap has it fallen into?

learn: The deadliest pattern when strategy fails is **escalation of commitment** — doubling down on a losing course precisely *because* you've already invested so much (sunk cost, now driven by ego and reputation). Our company isn't betting to win anymore; it's betting to avoid admitting it was wrong. That's how a recoverable mistake becomes a catastrophe.

The antidote starts with **honest, early recognition of failure** — which requires *pre-agreed signals* ('if we haven't hit X by Y, we stop or change') set while you're still rational, not in the heat of denial. Without tripwires, hope and ego will always argue for one more push.

When the course is wrong, the response is a **pivot** — a deliberate change of strategy while keeping what you've learned. The most celebrated companies often succeeded *after* pivoting hard from a failing original plan. A pivot isn't failure; refusing to pivot is.

This demands separating **decision from identity**. When leaders fuse their ego to a strategy, changing course feels like personal failure, so they don't — and the organisation pays. Cultures that treat changing your mind as strength (not weakness) make better, faster corrections.

And it requires a culture where people can **safely say 'this isn't working'** (the speak-up / no-blame thread from across GRC). If bad news about the strategy gets the messenger punished, leadership stays blind until the wall — exactly how escalation of commitment runs unchecked to the end.
bridge: So at work, you can guard against the most expensive strategic failure mode with two moves: set **pre-agreed tripwires** for when you'll stop or pivot (decided while calm), and build a culture where saying 'this strategy isn't working' is rewarded, not punished. Doubling down to avoid admitting you were wrong is how a turnable mistake becomes a shipwreck — the brave move is the turn.

interactions.title: Doubling down on a loser
interactions.prompt: A clearly-failing strategy gets more money poured in each time to 'prove it right'. What trap is this?

options.label: Healthy persistence — winners never quit.
options.feedback: This isn't persistence; it's escalation of commitment. They're investing to avoid admitting error, not to win. 'Never quit' applied to a sinking strategy is how a mistake becomes a catastrophe.

options.label: Escalation of commitment — doubling down to avoid admitting it was wrong.
options.feedback: Exactly. Sunk cost plus ego drives throwing more at a losing course. The fix is pre-agreed tripwires and a willingness to pivot — separating the decision from leaders' identity.

options.label: Fine, as long as they believe in it strongly.
options.feedback: Strong belief in a failing strategy is the problem, not the solution. Conviction without tripwires is exactly what fuels escalation of commitment to the bitter end.

interactions.title: Course-correct or escalate?
interactions.prompt: Sort each response to a failing strategy.

items.label: Pivoting to a new approach using what you learned
Pouring in more money to prove you were right
Pre-agreed tripwires that trigger a rethink
Punishing anyone who says it isn't working
Treating changing your mind as a strength

teachBack: Explain it to a friend in one line: “The deadliest strategic failure is doubling down on a losing course to avoid admitting you were wrong (escalation of commitment) — so set tripwires for when to pivot, separate decisions from ego, and reward people who say 'this isn't working'.”

options: Doubling down on a failing course because of what you've already invested
checkpoint.explain: Sunk cost plus ego drives more investment in a loser — turning a recoverable mistake into a catastrophe.

options: A deliberate change of strategy that keeps what you've learned
An admission you should never have started
checkpoint.explain: Pivoting isn't failure — refusing to pivot from a failing course is.

options: Lock you into the strategy forever
Decide when to stop or change while you're still rational, before denial sets in
checkpoint.explain: Set in the calm, tripwires force a rethink before hope and ego argue for 'one more push'.
